title: High Density Rechargeable Lithium-Ion Batteries Self-Assembled from Graphite Oxide Nanoplatelets and Polyelectrolytes
abstract:
High Density Rechargeable Lithium-Ion
Batteries Self-Assembled from Graphite Oxide
Nanoplatelets and Polyelectrolytes**
By Thierry Cassagneau and Janos H. Fendler*
The high storage capacity per volume of rechargeable
lithium-ion batteries has rendered them the commercial
choice for such consumer electronic products as camcor-
ders, cameras, and digital watches.[1±4] Shuttling lithium
ions between a lithium-metal negative electrode and an in-
tercalating compound serving as the positive electrode, or
alternatively between two intercalating electrodes (the so-
called rocking-chair battery), is the principle of the lithium-
ion battery.[1±4] The electrochemical potential difference
between the two intercalating electrodes determines the
storable voltage for a given lithium-ion battery and the
storage capacity is governed by the number of lithium ions
that can be reversibly intercalated into a given electrode
volume. Attempts to improve the storage capacity of the
Sony battery (Li-ion shuttling between a layered LiCoO2
cathode and a proprietary carbon anode) included the con-
struction of cells that used high-capacity carbonaceous ma-
terials and graphite as the intercalation cathode.[5±8] In
principle, assuming that lithium atoms occupy next-nearest
neighbor sites between each carbon sheet in graphite, one
lithium ion can be inserted per six carbon atoms (i.e.,
Li1C6) to produce a carbon anode with a specific capacity
of 372 mAh g±1. Further, if this capacity could be realized,
it would be possible to construct a rechargeable C/LiCoO2
battery with energies between 100 and 150 Wh kg±1 and
energy densities between 250 and 400 Wh L±1.[3] Although
carbonaceous electrodes with Li2C6 stoichiometry have
been prepared, most of these cells suffered from large hys-
teresis.[5,9]
We launched an entirely new approach to the construc-
tion of a high-capacity intercalating cathode by self-assem-
bling[10±15] nanometer-thick polyelectrolytes and graphite
oxide/graphite
nanoplatelets
onto
a
conducting
sub-
strate.[16] There are many advantages to our approach.
First, the ultrathin layers of polyelectrolytes and exfoliated
graphite oxide provide an unprecedentedly large surface
area per volume for the penetration of lithium ions. Indeed,
recent density of states calculations demonstrated that
graphene sheets of ca. 0.7 nm thickness could provide the
highest density storage (with a Li4C6 stoichiometry).[17]
Furthermore, modification of the graphite by oxidation (to
graphite oxide) results in the formation of nucleophilic
sites (carboxyl or hydroxyl groups, for example), which fa-
cilitate the interaction with positively charged polyelectro-
lytes, while the epitaxial adsorption of polymers, such as
poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO), occurs preferentially in the
unmodified graphitic domains.[18] Finally, nanostructuring
provides a molecular level control of the electrode compo-
sition, which, in turn, permits a systematic variation of dif-
ferent parameters and the elucidation of the charge storage
mechanism.
In the present communication, we report the construc-
tion of a high density (1232 mAh g±1 of graphitic carbon),
rechargeable lithium-ion battery based on a self-assembled
PEO, poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA),
and graphite oxide (GO) nanoplatelet cathode; a lithium
wire anode (and counter electrode); and LiASF6 electro-
lytes in methyl formate/ethylene carbonate (Fig. 1).
Ultrathin films of S-(PDDA/GO)10 (Film I), S-PDDA-
(GO/PEO)10 (Film II), S-(PDDA/GO/PEO)10 (Film III),
S-(PDDA/PEO)10 (Film IV), and S-(PDDA/MO/PEO)10
(Film V)[19], where S = substrate, were self-assembled and
characterized by absorption spectroscopy, cyclic voltamme-
try, voltage versus capacity plots (charging and discharg-
ing), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and atomic force micros-
copy (AFM). The regular growth of the films was estab-
lished by the observance of linear plots of the absorbances
at 230 nm against the number of sandwich units self-as-
sembled (Fig. 2). Modification of the topologies and thick-
nesses of the GO layers was assessed by AFM (Fig. 3).
Average thicknesses of 80 ± 12  for Film I and 70 ± 12 
for Film III were determined for the GO layer on top of
the polyelectrolytes, with a significant coverage (see Ta-
ble 1). However, an average thickness of only 20 ± 7  and
a lower coverage were observed for GO layered onto PEO
(Film II, Table 1).
Charging and discharging were determined by standard
voltage versus capacity measurements (Fig. 4) in an elec-
trochemical cell (Fig. 1). In all cases, full reversibility of the
charging processes was observed, but interestingly, the
main plateaus appeared at lower potentials during the cur-
rent discharge (ca. +0.3 V vs. Li/Li+) than during the cur-
rent charge (ca. +0.7 V vs. Li/Li+), indicating that these
two processes may be governed by different mechanisms.
In spite of the fact that Film I contained the largest amount
of GO, the charging was the smallest (Table 1), indicating
that using a positively charged electrolyte (PDDA) hin-
dered the insertion of Li+ ions. In order to improve the pen-
etration of Li+ ions into the multilayers, each GO layer
was sandwiched between a layer of PDDA and a layer of
PEO (Film III) or, alternatively, GO was sandwiched be-
tween PEO only and self-assembled onto a PDDA-coated
substrate (Film II). The very high specific capacities of
Films II and III (Table 1) are likely to originate in the fa-
cilitated transport of Li+ to the vicinity of the GO nano-
platelets by PEO. The activity of these GO layers was also

demonstrated by determining the charging capacity of a
film in which GO was replaced by montmorillonite (MO)
platelets (Film V). Charging of Film V, relative to Film IV,
was found to be negligible, demonstrating the crucial im-
portance of GO in the intercalation of lithium ions. Current
charging for all five films is summarized in Table 1.
For the sake of convenience the capacity was expressed
in mA min per cm2 of working electrode. Evaluation of the
charging properties of the different electrodes required a
knowledge of the amount of GO present in each film.
Based upon the values of the thicknesses and coverages
evaluated from AFM images, the amount of graphitic car-
bon in each multilayer can be deduced by means of Equa-
tion 1, which allowed us to formally extract the contribu-
tion from the graphitic layers. Here, NC corresponds to the
number of graphitic carbon atoms in an area of 1 cm2 of
working electrode, NA is Avogadro's number, TGO
seq is the
thickness of each layer of GO in , dGO
001 is the interlayer
spacing deduced from the XRD pattern of the oxidized
powder prior to exfoliation (3.66 ), A(unit cell) is the area
in 2 of a hexagonal GO unit (5.24 2 with C±C distance
of 1.42 ), and the factor 2 accounts for the number of car-
bon atoms per unit cell.

By assuming that charging of one electron by the work-
ing electrode is followed by the insertion of one Li+ ion,
the amount of lithium ion intercalated subsequent to the
cell reaching zero potential is easily obtained from the sim-
ple relation described in Equation 2, where Q corresponds
to the stored charge in coulombs, and F is the Faraday con-
stant.
NLi  Q
F
(2)
The results obtained for the five films are reported in Ta-
ble 1. Uncertainties in the evaluation of the coverage and
of the topology of the entire film surface necessitated an-
other approach to the determination of the amount of ac-
tive GO involved in the charging process. This was done by
using the extinction coefficient of a 2.6 ´ 10±4 M colloidal
suspension of GO (e(230) = 21 412 M±1 cm±1) to deduce the
effective contribution of GO layers from the corresponding
absorption spectrum of a given film by means of rewriting
Equation 1 (see Eq. 3). Here TGO
film corresponds to the total
Fig. 1. a) Schematic of the self-assembled
rechargeable lithium-ion battery used for
the electrical measurements. b) Schematic
of a self-assembled S-(PDDA/GO/PEO)3
film. The box illustrates the intercalation of
lithium ions into the GO and PEO layers
(i.e., charging and concomitant reduction of
GO to G).

Batteries Self-Assembled from Graphite Oxide
Nanoplatelets and Polyelectrolytes**
By Thierry Cassagneau and Janos H. Fendler*

tion of a high density (1232 mAh g±1 of graphitic carbon),

and graphite oxide (GO) nanoplatelet cathode; a lithium

lytes in methyl formate/ethylene carbonate (Fig. 1).

(GO/PEO)10 (Film II), S-(PDDA/GO/PEO)10 (Film III),
S-(PDDA/PEO)10 (Film IV), and S-(PDDA/MO/PEO)10
(Film V)[19], where S = substrate, were self-assembled and
characterized by absorption spectroscopy, cyclic voltamme-
try, voltage versus capacity plots (charging and discharg-

copy (AFM). The regular growth of the films was estab-
lished by the observance of linear plots of the absorbances
at 230 nm against the number of sandwich units self-as-
sembled (Fig. 2). Modification of the topologies and thick-
nesses of the GO layers was assessed by AFM (Fig. 3).

ble 1). However, an average thickness of only 20 ± 7  and
a lower coverage were observed for GO layered onto PEO

rent discharge (ca. +0.3 V vs. Li/Li+) than during the cur-
rent charge (ca. +0.7 V vs. Li/Li+), indicating that these
two processes may be governed by different mechanisms.

dered the insertion of Li+ ions. In order to improve the pen-
etration of Li+ ions into the multilayers, each GO layer

PEO (Film III) or, alternatively, GO was sandwiched be-
tween PEO only and self-assembled onto a PDDA-coated
substrate (Film II). The very high specific capacities of

cilitated transport of Li+ to the vicinity of the GO nano-

ble 1. Uncertainties in the evaluation of the coverage and
of the topology of the entire film surface necessitated an-
other approach to the determination of the amount of ac-
tive GO involved in the charging process. This was done by
using the extinction coefficient of a 2.6 ´ 10±4 M colloidal
suspension of GO (e(230) = 21 412 M±1 cm±1) to deduce the
effective contribution of GO layers from the corresponding
absorption spectrum of a given film by means of rewriting
Equation 1 (see Eq. 3). Here TGO
Fig. 1. a) Schematic of the self-assembled
rechargeable lithium-ion battery used for
the electrical measurements. b) Schematic
of a self-assembled S-(PDDA/GO/PEO)3
film. The box illustrates the intercalation of
lithium ions into the GO and PEO layers
(i.e., charging and concomitant reduction of
thickness of GO layers for a given numbers of sequences
In spite of the shortcomings and assumptions made, the
thicknesses calculated for the GO layers in a given film by
these two approaches agreed fairly well (the thickness of
one GO layer in Film I was determined to be 64.0  (aver-
age peak-to-valley distance times coverage) by AFM and
film divided by the number of sequences) by ab-
sorption spectroscopy; that in Film III was 56.0  by AFM
and 55.5  by absorption spectroscopy (see Table 1).
Concomitant with lithium-ion insertion, GO was found
to reduce to graphite, G. Conversion of GO to G occurred
at a potential of +2.6 V vs. Li/Li+, as determined by cyclic
voltammetry. It should be noted that charging started at a
less positive potential (+2.2 V, Fig. 4). The contribution of
PEO was evaluated to be 0.105 mA min/cm2 by comparing
the charging (or discharging) of Film IV and Film V. Sub-
tracting this contribution from the charging determined for
Film II and Film III resulted in a linear relationship be-
tween charging and the amount of GO incorporated into
the films (the ratio of {Q[C]/cm2 ± 0.105}/{total thickness of
the GO layers} was almost identical for Film II and Film III:
8.5 ´ 10±5 and 8.7 ´ 10±5, respectively).
Cycling of our battery was tested for Film III (Fig. 5).
Voltage versus capacity curves for lithium-ion intercala-
tion±deintercalation cycles were observed to stabilize after
the second cycle with no significant charging losses, and cy-
cles in some cases exactly superimposed on each other (See
Insertion of lithium ions during the charging caused a
dramatic change in the absorption spectra of the films: they
became progressively blacker (Fig. 2b), paralleling the GO
to G conversion. Importantly, more than 92 % of the charg-
ing and 73 % of the discharging occurred at a potential be-
low 0.65 V for Film II and Film III, making them particular-
ly attractive as candidates for low-voltage rechargeable
batteries. Most remarkably, values of the reversible specific
capacity of the graphite layers in these films (1174 mAh g±1
and 1232 mAh g±1, Table 1) were much higher than the
highest specific capacities (500±770 mAh g±1)[19,20] reported
for carbonaceous materials and in the range of values
measured for polyacenic semiconductor (PAS) materials
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0935-9648/98/1108-0879 $ 17.50+.50/0
Fig. 2. a) Linear dependence of the absorbance, monitored at 230 nm, on the
number of sequences self-assembled in Film I, Film II, and Film III, where the
squares, circles, and triangles indicate data for GO, PDDA, and PEO, respec-
tively. b) Variation of the optical absorption of the Film III as a function of
wavelength in a discharged, partially charged, and fully charged state.
Fig. 3. AFM image of a GO layer standing
on top of a poly(ethylene oxide) layer in
the system S-(PDDA/GO/PEO)n. Also
shown, a close view of a GO nanoplatelet
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0935-9648/98/1108-0880 $ 17.50+.50/0
(1100 mAh g±1).[21] However, these cells suffered from pro-
nounced (ca. 1.0 V) hysteresis[5,9,20,21] and/or important irre-
versible capacity (23% of the full capacity for PAS materi-
als).[21b] The batteries prepared here (based on Film II and
Film III) exhibited a significant hysteresis with respect to
potential but had fully reversible capacity even on the first
charging. We attribute this high-performance capacity to
the presence of stacked graphite nanoplatelets (with high
surface areas) that are in contact with PEO (Fig. 1). A lithi-
um to carbon ratio of 1:2 (compound LiC2) is only achiev-
able with small diameter graphene sheets,[5,17] suggesting
that the oxidative treatment leading to GO generated im-
portant edge modifications and disruptions of the conjuga-
tion length, leading to smaller graphitic domains. Using
PEO)10 electrode (Film III) prepared in this work, we have
discovered a new route to the nanofabrication of fully
reversible Li-ion batteries. Furthermore, our electrochemi-
cal cell can be readily scaled up to an economically viable
battery. Obtaining the colloid and surface chemical under-
standing necessary for the realization of this goal is the
subject of our current intensive scrutiny.
Table 1. Properties of the different self-assembled electrodes.
[a] Calculated values based on thickness determined by AFM (top) and by absorption spectra (bottom). [b] Cal-
culated from absorption spectra, taking e230 nm = 21 412 M±1 cm±1. [c] Calculated from the charging process.
Calculated for the graphitic layers only, by taking into account the amount of carbon deduced from absorption
spectroscopy. [e] In good agreement with the AFM image histogram of a window of 1 mm2, indicating a maximum
distribution at 85 . [f] This thickness corresponds to a GO layer sandwiched between PEO layers only. [g] In
good agreement with the AFM image histogram of a window of 1 mm2, indicating a maximum distribution at
25 . [h] By assuming that PEO layers contribute 0.060 C/cm2.
Fig. 4. Current charge and discharge curves obtained for the five self-
assembled systems, relative to a lithium reference electrode.
Fig. 5. Voltage±capacity curves for the first seven intercalation±deintercala-
tion cycles (0.05 mA/s) of Film III, S-(PDDA/GO/PEO)10. Inset shows the
cycle life behavior for 10 cycles.
Poly(ethylene oxide), (Mw = 300 000, PEO), poly(diallyldimethylammo-
nium) chloride (20 wt.-%, PDDA), graphite powder (1±2 mm diameter), so-
dium chlorate (99+ %), LiAsF6 (98 %), anhydrous methyl formate (99 %),
and montmorillonite KSF were purchased from Aldrich; ethylene carbonate
(Fluka), lithium wires (3.2 mm in diameter, Alfa), and nitric acid (15.8 N,
Fisher) were used as received. All aqueous solutions were prepared from
18 MW cm water obtained by passing distilled water through a Millipore
Milli-Q membrane filtration system.
Graphite oxide, GO, was prepared by a slight modification of the pub-
lished procedure [16,22]. Briefly, the oxidation procedure was repeated
twice by recycling the graphite after each step. Graphite powder was charac-
terized by XRD prior and subsequent to each oxidation. Typically, the inter-
layer spacing, initially close to 3.34 , shifted to 3.49  and 3.66  after the
first and second oxidation steps, respectively. Oxidation of graphite was
monitored by FTIR measurement in diffuse reflectance mode. After the last
oxidation step, important changes were observed in the spectrum, with ab-
sorptions at 3362 cm±1 (nOH); 1707 cm±1 (keto group, strong); 1370 cm±1
(very strong), which can be assigned to ns(C=O); and 827 cm±1 (weak), possi-
bly due to dsc(O±C=O). Additionally, absorption bands at 1646 cm±1
(strong) and 1560 cm±1 were observed, which could be attributed to
nas(C=O) and n(C=C) modes, respectively. The bare graphite was character-
ized by absorption bands at 1546 cm±1 due to C=C bonds, and 1086 cm±1.
Elemental analysis (Galbraith Laboratories Inc.) of the GO powder, after
the second oxidation step for C12HO3, resulted in: C = 74.32 %, H <0.54 %,
O = 25.14 % (calculated: C= 74.6 %, H = 0.54 %, O = 24.85 %). The GO
powder was dispersed in 0.3 M NaCl aqueous solution by stirring for 20 h.
After centrifugation, a fresh slightly colored colloidal suspension of GO was
obtained and used in the self-assembly for a period of 2 days.
Indium tin oxide (ITO) coated glass slides (1 ´ 1 cm2) or quartz sub-
strates (1 ´ 1 cm2) were exposed for 3 min to a mixture H2SO4(75)/
H2O2(25) v/v, and carefully washed with deionized water prior to use. For
self-assembly these substrates were immersed into a 10±3 M PDDA solution
(pH 6.5) for 4 min, followed by thorough rinsing with deionized water and
drying under a nitrogen flow. Next, the PDDA-coated substrate was im-
mersed into the GO dispersion for 20 min, rinsed, and dried. This procedure
produced a sandwich layer of PDDA and GO on a substrate, S-(PDDA)/
GO [19]. Subsequent layers were self-assembled analogously. In some cases
PEO was self-assembled by dipping S-(PDDA) into a 10±4 M PEO solution
(pH 7.0) for 20 min. Again, subsequent layers of (PDDA)/PEO were self-
assembled analogously. Montmorillonite, MO (0.2 wt.-% of exfoliated dis-
persion), was used to prepare the films, with typical dipping times of 10 s
[23]. The growth of the sequence PDDA/MO/PEO was monitored by UV-
vis adsorption and evidenced by the regular increase of the absorption
bands at 196 nm and 280 nm after each deposition cycle (the colloidal sus-
pension of MO exhibited absorption bands at 208 nm and 296 nm).
X-ray diffraction measurements were performed on a Siemens K710 X-
ray diffractometer. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Mattson Instruments
Model 2020 Galaxy Series FTIR spectrometer, and absorption spectra were
taken on a Hewlett-Packard 8252A instrument. AFM images were taken
using a Topometrix Explorer 2000 scanning probe microscope in non-con-
tact mode with standard silicon nitride tips (force constant of 0.12 N/m). A
JEOL JEM-1200EXII electron microscope, operating at 120 kV, was used
to characterize the layered structure of GO after centrifugation. Chronopo-
tentiometry was conducted under nitrogen atmosphere at a constant current
of 0.05 mA with a potentiostat (EG&G Princeton Applied Research, Model
273) interfaced with a computer running with the Head Start software. The
working electrode consisted of self-assembled active multilayers laid down
onto ITO glass substrate, the reference and auxiliary electrode being lithium
wires (Fig. 1). Charging and discharging of the multilayers was performed
between 2.5 V and a value close to 0 V, while the data were collected from
the potentiostat. The electrolytic medium consisted of a mixture of ethylene
0.5:0.8:0.003 molar ratio. The values of charging and discharging obtained
for the different systems were averaged over 2±3 different samples for each
system, with a deviation of about 4 %.

J. Electrochem. Soc. 1995, 142, 2581.
[6] J. R. Dahn, T. Zheng, Y. H. Liu, J. S. Xue, Science 1995, 270, 590.
[7] Y. H. Liu, J. S. Xue, T. Zheng, J. R. Dahn, Carbon 1996, 34, 193.
[8] J. R. Dahn, W. Xing, Y. Gao, Carbon 1997, 35, 825.
[9] W. B. Xing, J. R. Dahn, J. Electrochem. Soc. 1997, 144, 1195.
[10] S. W. Keller, H. N. Kim, T. E. Mallouk, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 1994, 116,
[11] S. W. Keller, S. A. Johnson, E. S. Brigham, E. H. Yonemoto, T. E. Mal-
